head.ce:title: Enhancement effect of biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the performance of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs)

ce:section.ce:para: The exploration of alternative energy sources has been increasing due to higher fossil fuel consumption to meet the global energy demand. Various renewable energy sources, for instance, harvesting photons via solar energy, can be a possible approach to reduce fossil fuel consumption (Moheimani and Parlevliet, 2013). Solar technologies based on silicon solar cells (SSCs) were first considered as an attractive alternative for photon harvesting. However, several challenges associated with its efficiency, production, reliability, and quality make these techniques more complicated (Kang et al., 2022). Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), an unconventional technology, offer a promising alternative with their photovoltaic and electrochemical properties (Hu et al., 2014), capable of converting solar energy into electricity without any emissions (Zhu et al., 2017). First discovered by O'Regan and Grätzel in 1991 (O'regan and Grätzel, 1991), DSSCs are recognized as novel and third-generation solar cells (Babar et al., 2020), imitating the photosynthesis phenomena occurring within plants (Grätzel, 2003). These unconventional solar cells are popular due to their lower costs, easy fabrication, availability of abundant raw materials, technical advancements, moderate energy conversion efficiency, and environmentally friendly applications (Yen et al., 2009; Zamiri and Bagheri, 2018). It can outperform conventional SSCs and are particularly effective under diffused sunlight and comprises several components: photoanodes (TiO2), redox couple (electrolyte solution), sensitizer (dye), and finally platinum counter electrode (CE) (Li et al., 2021; Teymourinia et al., 2018). The photoanodes (TiO2) and sensitizers in DSSCs are the major components for photon capture and electron injection (Amir-Al Zumahi et al., 2021). Unlike SSCs, the photosensitizing dye molecules in DSSCs are key components, as they are sufficiently reactive and efficient in capturing and collecting photons and converting them into electricity under diffused sunlight (Zhang et al., 2019b).

DSSCs, considered the most promising photovoltaic systems, however, still face potential challenges associated with their photovoltaic power conversion efficiencies (PCE), hindering their large-scale applications (Kang et al., 2022; Teymourinia et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019b). Several researchers have studied and reported the science behind the problem of lower PCE value (Teymourinia et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2021). The energy inconsistencies among photons and band gap of photoanodes or sensitizers will lead to low light harvesting, ultimately resulting in lower PCE values (Liu et al., 2021). A study reported that charge recombination among sensitizers and photoanodes is one of the major problems resulting in lower performance in DSSCs (Jahantigh et al., 2020a). Another study also suggested that low light absorption resulted in lower PCE values for DSSCs (Teymourinia et al., 2018). Several prospects for better light harvesting to maximize the working efficiency of DSSCs have been explored: application of novel sensitizers/co-sensitizers (Gao et al., 2021; Tapa et al., 2021), altering photoanodes (Ahmad et al., 2022), modification of CE (Xu and Jin, 2020), and infusion of noble metals with carbon dots (CDs) (Teymourinia et al., 2018). Wante et al. (2021) modified the surface properties of the photoanode by plasma treatment and fabricated it with the DSSCs, which significantly increased the PCE value from 0.94 % to 2.25 %. Another study designed a nanostructured CE material, by decorating Fe3O4 nanoflowers with Ni nanoparticles, and wrapped them with reduced graphene oxides (Fe3O4/Ni@rGO) which improved both the stability and better performance of DSSCs (Xu and Jin, 2020). Recently, different types of organic and inorganic dyes have garnered more attention and are being extensively used as sensitizers/co-sensitizers as another alternative approach to enhance PCE (Wu et al., 2020). However, the maximum PCE value achieved by using these dyes was below 4%, suggesting other potential alternatives (Bashir et al., 2018).

Various carbonaceous organic materials with higher carbon content like CDs, graphene, graphene oxides, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), rGO, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), etc., are mainly used for photoanodes modification to improve the DSSCs performance (Ahmad et al., 2022; Kumar et al., 2019, 2023). Numerous physiochemical properties of GQDs and CDs, such as higher solubility and photostability, better light absorbance, and ease of modification make them one of the most effective carbonaceous doping materials to supplement the photoanode qualities in DSSCs (Rezaei et al., 2019). Co-sensitization of GQDs and other organic dyes in several major components of DSSCs has been widely used to maximize the ability of photovoltaic cells to convert light into energy (Kundu et al., 2017; Mahalingam et al., 2022). Nevertheless, several problems of GQDs are associated with their poor conductivity (Tian et al., 2018), unknown specific dimensions (Anwer et al., 2023), and, most importantly, their inability to absorb light in the visible region (Jahantigh et al., 2020b) will result in their lower efficiency for charge separation and collection in DSSCs.

CQDs, unlike other QDs, have high luminesce, dispersibility in water, and chemical stability, which makes them suitable for their application as alternative light absorbers (Li et al., 2015a). Higher photostability (Liu et al., 2017), intrinsic conductivity (Devadas and Imae, 2018), higher surface deactivation, and simple functionalization are other additional features of CQDs. Several studies have recommended the application of colloidal CQDs because of their environmentally friendly properties, low synthesis costs, higher absorption coefficients, and greater quantum yields (Zhao et al., 2018, 2021). Incorporation of CQDs in photoanodes will establish a carbon/semiconductor symbiotic system, resulting in relatively faster electron transport, with better dye adsorption and reduced recombination rate, ultimately enhancing the DSSC performance (Mehmood et al., 2015). Liu et al. (2019) suggested CQDs addition (CQDs < 10 nm) resulted in the tunable energy band gap that matched and covered the bandgap range for solar cells CQDs are excellent carbonaceous materials with several advantages. However, the production costs for its synthesis on a larger scale still seem to be a practical challenge.

The prospects of using biomass-derived CQD as an unconventional approach to overcome the several challenges associated with CQDs’ production costs have previously been reported (Meng et al., 2017; Ramanan et al., 2016; Tang et al., 2017). Biomass-derived CQDs are generally extracted from naturally occurring substances with a vast carbon content, like carbohydrates that include glucose, sucrose, and fructose (Meng et al., 2017). Previous studies reported several eco-friendly methods to synthesize CQDs from different sources: algal blooms, orange peels, sugarcane molasses, and lignin (Ding et al., 2018; Ramanan et al., 2016). The precursor and extraction methods predict the electrochemical, optical, and photoelectric properties, including the presence and different types of functional groups within biomass-derived CQDs (Gao et al., 2020). Meng et al. (2017) suggested that soyabeans-derived CQDs were used to construct all-weather solar cells. Another study used water hyacinth (Eichhornia crassipes) as a precursor to synthesize CQDs and fabricated it with DSSCs, which yielded a better fill factor of 67% (Yang et al., 2018). Thus, biomass-derived CQDs have been shown to be one of the effective sources with excellent optoelectronic characteristics to enhance the performance of DSSCs as suggested in the previous studies.

In this regard, our study reports the application of CQDs derived from corn stover as a co-sensitizer along with the organic dyes for better DSSCs performance. A novel and eco-friendly method was used to synthesize CQDs from corn stover by a simple hydrothermal technique without additional chemical agents at a low temperature. For the first time, the corn stover-derived CQDs were used as a co-sensitizer to improve DSSC performance.

The detailed experimental procedures, including materials and chemical reagents, characterization techniques, photoanode fabrication and DSSCs assembly, synthesis of biomass-derived CQDs, and power conversion efficiency calculation, are provided in supplementary data.

Fig. 1 (a) represents the UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs. It exhibited a peak at 232 nm, representing the π-π* transition of the C=C bond, while the peak at 280 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of the C=O or C=N bonds (Li et al., 2015b; Lu et al., 2021b). These are the typical features of the CQDs derived from biomass (Raikwar, 2022). In the 3D-EEM spectra of the sample (Fig. 1(b)), the energy profiles are represented by different colors, with red being the highest, orange, and black being the least. It indicated that the CQDs product had a peak (λEX/λEM = 380–400 nm/460–480 nm). CQDs showed obvious blue fluorescence when irradiated at UV-light (365 nm) (Lu et al., 2021b). These results showed that the synthesized CQDs had excellent photoluminescence effects (Qin et al., 2020).

The prepared CQDs’ HR-TEM image and size distribution are shown in Appendix A Fig. S2 (a) and (b), respectively. The image suggested that the CQDs were nearly spherical, and their size ranged from 3 to 6.5 nm, with an average diameter of 4.63 ± 0.87 nm, as evidenced by the random statistics of 100 counts. Fig. 2 (a) and (b) shows the top view SEM images of TiO2 and TiO2+CQDs films. Both films exhibited uniform surface morphology. The cross-sectional image of the TiO2 film shown in Fig. 2(c), indicates an average film thickness of 13.74 ± 0.07 µm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface structural and chemical composition analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The changes in the crystallization of TiO2 and the phase of their products after CQDs doping were investigated using XRD. The XRD patterns of bare TiO2 and TiO2 + CQDs are illustrated in Appendix A Fig. S3. The pristine TiO2 photoanode exhibited diffraction peaks at 2θ were 25.9°, 48.6°, 54.6° 55.7°, 63.2°, and 69.5° (Thambiliyagodage and Usgodaarachchi, 2021; Yan et al., 2019; Yurdakal et al., 2013), which were assigned to planes (101), (200), (105), (211), (204), and (116), respectively, of the pure anatase as per JCPDS card No. 21–1272 (Yan et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2009). Referring to Fig. S3, TiO2+CQDs diffraction peak positions and intensity remain unchanged compared to TiO2 nanocrystals, indicating that the TiO2 crystal structure was unaffected by CQDs addition. However, a weak new peak is observed around 2θ = 28° on CQDs incorporated sample, which could be due to the interaction of CQDs with TiO2 nanoparticles (Saikia et al., 2022).

The surface chemical compositions and interactions of TiO2 and their composite were investigated using XPS. It is a prominent method for analyzing atomic elemental compositions and surface chemical state changes in materials (Cong et al., 2011; Dong et al., 2023; Paneru et al., 2020). The high-resolution C1 s and O1 s spectra are shown in Fig. 3; bare TiO2 (Fig. 3 (a) and (b)) and TiO2 + CQDs (Fig. 3(c) and (d)). The C1 s peak of bare TiO2 consisted of three main peaks positioned at 285 eV, 285.4 eV, and 289.1 eV, which are attributed to CC (Lu et al., 2021a; Tak et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2020), CO/COH (Nie et al., 2021; Ren et al., 2014; Shilpa et al., 2023) and OC = O (Wu et al., 2020; Yuferov et al., 2022) respectively. The peak centered at 286.7 eV (Gogoi et al., 2020; Venkatesh et al., 2020; Yuferov et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2019), 288.6 eV (Jian et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2020; Yuferov et al., 2022), and 289.6 eV (Aliyeva et al., 2019) appeared in CQDs doped TiO2 photoanode which belongs to CO/C = O/CNH, OC = O/NC = O, and C=O respectively suggesting that these polar functional groups might have appeared due to CQDs addition. These results were also supported by the N1 s spectra (Appendix A Fig. S4 (b)), where the peaks located at 400.4 eV, 400.8 eV, and 402.5 eV correspond to N-(C)3 (Huang et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2012), NH (Gao et al., 2023), and N-X (Dong et al., 2023), respectively. These peaks were observed in TiO2+CQDs. However, the bare TiO2 photoanodes showed no distinct peaks (Appendix A Fig. S4 (c)). Thus, the above XPS analysis confirmed the self-doping nature of CQDs. The O1 s core level spectrum for both samples showed major peaks at 530.4 eV and 531.8 eV, attributed to Ti-O (Munoz et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2021), and Ti-OH (Wei et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2020a) respectively. In TiO2+CQDs samples, a new peak, O = C, was observed at 532.7 eV after CQDs addition (Jiang et al., 2014; Qin and Zeng, 2017), which can also be ascribed to the oxygen rich functional group of CQDs. Since the C-Ti bond at the binding energy ∼281 eV in XPS spectrum did not appear in TiO2+CQDs photoanodes, implying that the main interaction occurred between CQDs and TiO2 via the Ti-O-C bond (Liu et al., 2014; Martins et al., 2016). This interaction could be more favorable to promoting the electron transfer between them as reported in previous studies (Martins et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2019a). In addition, the deconvoluted Ti 2p spectrum displayed in Appendix A Fig. S4a, the TiO2+CQDs sample exhibited slightly higher binding energy peaks at 465.03 eV and 459.31 eV, for Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 compared to that of bare TiO2, further suggesting the possible interactions among CQDs and TiO2.

The electrochemical properties of the photoanodes were evaluated using several methods, like EIS, CV, M-S, etc. Chen et al. (2012) suggested that a small improvement in interfacial charge resistance in photoanode can improve DSSC performance. Previous studies have also reported that the modification on the electrode materials can also result in the better performance of DSSCs (Liang et al., 2017; Mamba et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2020, 2024). A study found that the application of ultrathin MoS2 nanofilms doped in TiO2 nanoparticles coated in carbon fiber resulted in higher electrochemical activity of DSSCs (Liang et al., 2017). Xu et al. (2024) developed a high-performance CE for DSSCs by synthesizing a heterostructure nanoreactor consisting of MoC/MoO2 nanodots (NDs) encapsulated in N-doped carbon nanoflowers (NCF), which resulted in faster electron transfer process, improved structural stability ultimately resulting in longer redox reactions. Another study fabricated NiFe2O4/BiPO4 nanocomposites, where the pairing of photo-generated electrons from BiPO4 with holes in NiFe2O4 resulted in an enhanced charge carrier extraction and utilization during photo-excitation (Koutavarapu et al., 2021). Thus, the photoanode modifications can result in better charge transport and EIS was utilized to understand the charge transport kinetics within the electron transport layer of the photoanode. Fig. 4 (a) represents the Nyquist plot where the X-intercept of the left semicircle denotes the internal resistance (R S), which exhibits the device's electrical conductivity (Chen et al., 2012), and the charge transport resistance (R CT) is represented by the diameter of the arc (Dahal et al., 2020; Pandey and Jeong, 2023). The modified Randal's equivalent circuit (using Z-fitting software) is depicted in the inset of Fig. 4(a). Adding CQDs to the electrode has no major impact on the internal resistance (Fig. 4(a)). However, the charge transfer resistance of CQDs doped TiO2 with Di-tetrabutylammonium cis-bis(isothiocyanato)bis(2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicarboxylato)ruthenium(II)[N719] is 19.60 Ω, notably smaller than those recorded for N719 dyed TiO2 (44.38 Ω) and bare TiO2 (71.36 Ω). The results obtained from this study disclose that incorporating CQDs in the TiO2 photoanode substantially enhanced the charge transport activities at the electrode-electrolyte interface, resulting in a significant decrease in charge transfer resistance compared to undoped photoanode in DSSC. The reduction in R CT was a result of better chemical bonding among CQDs and TiO2, which could potentially prevent direct electron recombination. This similar phenomenon has also been reported by Rezaei et al. (Rezaei et al., 2019). Moreover, the presence of CQDs between dye molecules and the semiconductor interface provides a relatively faster and more efficient transport channel to the charge carriers (Jahantigh et al., 2020a).

The M-S plot was used to assess more information on how well the samples enable electron migration, as reflected by the flat band potential (Vfb ). The expression for the Vfb calculation is elucidated in Appendix A Section S5. The positive slope of the linear plots of all three photoanodes validates their classification as n-type semiconductors (Zhou et al., 2019, 2021) as depicted in Fig. 4(b). The observed Vfb values for bare TiO2, TiO2+N719, and TiO2+CQDs+N719 samples were approximately −1.30 V, −1.54 V, and −1.68 V versus Ag/AgCl, respectively. The Vfb shift to the more negative for the CQDs doped sample (−1.68 V) compared to the bare TiO2 (−1.30 V) suggests the fermi level is shifting upward (Kundu et al., 2017). This shift promotes charge transfer at the photoelectrode-electrolyte interface (Li et al., 2014; Zhou et al., 2020b), ultimately contributing to better DSSC performance (Xu et al., 2024). This outcome aligns more closely with the observed RCT value in the EIS test and is compelling evidence for a reduced charge transport barrier in the CQDs doped sample. Moreover, the slope observed for TiO₂ + CQDs + N719 is smaller than that for pristine TiO₂, indicating that the charge carrier density increases after CQDs doping. The higher electron density in CQDs doped sample improves the Fermi level towards the conduction band, resulting in enhanced electron transport. This result is also confirmed by the observed Vfb (Liang et al., 2016).

To further analyze the electrochemical properties of the CQDs co-sensitized device, the CV test was performed using a three-electrode system; (a) reference electrode (Ag/AgCl in 1 mol/L KCl), (b) CE (Pt sheet, 1 cm × 1 cm), and (c) working electrode (biomass-derived CQDs coated Pt/FTO). The typical CV curve and corresponding energy levels are illustrated in Fig. 4(c) and (d) respectively. Based on the CV curve (Admassie et al., 2006; Gupta et al., 2011), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy of CQDs is −3.89 eV, and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy is −5.48 eV (Appendix A Fig. S5) below vacuum level and is similar to those with a previous report (Shejale et al., 2021). The respective energy of CQDs acquired from the CV curve ranging between N719 and TiO2 conduction band/valence bands act as bridges, facilitating improved energy-level alignment (Gupta et al., 2011; Shejale et al., 2021), enabling electron transfer and injection of holes.

Under light illumination, the excited dye molecules generate photoinduced electrons. The operation of DSSCs relies on the interaction between various photoelectrode interfaces, especially at the junction of semiconductor and dye. Here, the excited dye molecules generate electrons, which travel through a three-dimensional network of semiconductor material to reach the collecting electrode, enabling final charge collection under favorable energy alignments (Chen et al., 2012). To ensure optimal performance of a DSSC, the light-induced charge carrier should transfer smoothly from the photoanode to the conductive substrate and ultimately reach the redox couple through an external circuit without any hindrance. Therefore, the photoanode should possess an efficient charge transport capability to prevent the loss of electrons induced by light illumination, thereby enhancing the overall performance of DSSCs.

Herein, the study systematically examined the impact of co-sensitization regarding light harvesting in DSSCs, with a specific focus on parameters including (a) open circuit voltage (V OC), (b) short circuit current density (J SC), (c) fill factor (FF), and (d) power conversion efficiency (PCE, η). The estimation of FF and η of the solar cells were provided in the Supplementary data S6. For each category (with/without CQDs photoanodes), five identical cells were prepared and reported the mean value to examine the effectiveness of CQDs as co-sensitizers: cells sensitizing with N719 dye only (TiO2+N719), and cells sensitizers containing CQDs and N719 (TiO2+CQDs+N719). Fig. 5 (a) illustrates the current density voltage (J-V) properties of DSSCs and the average value of J SC, V OC, FF, and η of the cells are presented in Table 1 . Evidently, the CQDs doped DSSC (TiO2+CQDs+N719) demonstrated higher J SC (12.69 ± 0.25 mA/cm2), V OC (0.70 ± 0.01 V), and η (6.08 ± 0.12 %) compared to TiO2 +N719 cell (J SC = 10.13 ± 0.33 mA/cm2, V OC = 0.67 ± 0.02 V, and η = 4.78 ± 0.17 %). The J SC of the CQDs incorporated cell showed a significant improvement (∼26%) compared to the TiO2+N719 cell. It is seen that the improvement of J SC in TiO2+CQDs+N719 leads to the PCE (η) value increasing by 27.20 % compared to TiO2+N719. The decrease in interfacial impedance between the photoanode and the electrolyte from the incorporation of the CQDs might have resulted in higher J SC. The proposed mechanism diagram for the enhanced performance of DSSCs due to CQDs incorporation is shown in Appendix A Fig. S7. Moreover, the enhancement of Jsc was attributed to the fluorescence properties of CQDs, which enhanced the photon emission to a visible region when the photoanode was irradiated with UV light (Cheruku et al., 2023), as suggested by 3D-EEM result. Additionally, the CQDs entered the free space of the TiO2 network and bonded with the TiO2 more effectively, which resulted in the ease of electron transport increasing the corresponding current density. These conclusions are supported by several other previous studies as shown in Table 2 .

As shown in Table 2, CQDs can be synthesized from different methods and used for different purposes: counter electrode, blocking layer, co-sensitizer, sensitizer electrolyte, etc. (Ghann et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2014; Mihalache et al., 2015; Mohan et al., 2018). Several studies have experimentally demonstrated the synergistic effects of CQDs that resulted in better visible light absorption, faster electron transfer, enhanced charge separation, suppressed electron-hole recombination, etc. ultimately resulting in better DSSCs performance with improved PCE values (Huang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018). Ghann et al. (2019) suggested that the synthesized CQDs had some sensitizing effects that improved the PCE value due to the electron transfer that occurred between N719 dye and CQDs. Another study conducted on ZnO/N719-based DSSCs concluded that CQDs prepared from ethylenediamine and citric acid (2:1 molar ratio) contributed to a much higher electron-hole pair separation, which resulted in a seven-fold increase in PCE value compared to that of DSSCs without CQDs (Efa and Imae, 2019). Adding CQDs in our experiment also resulted in an increase in Jsc from 10.13 mA/cm2 to 12.69 mA/cm2 which resulted due to a faster electron transfer under illumination (Efa and Imae, 2019). Increase in J SC also resulted in an increase in V OC values from 0.67 V to 0.7 V. The V OC of the cell is influenced by the energy level difference between the redox potentials of the electrolyte and the fermi energy level of the TiO2 photoanode as reported in previous studies (Chandiran et al., 2014; Janani et al., 2015; Shaikh et al., 2018). Previous studies have shown that TiO2 doped with carbon materials exhibits a negative shift in its conduction band edge and an upshift in the fermi energy level (Kang et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2017). This shift contributes to an elevation in the V OC of DSSCs. This phenomenon is further supported by the negative shift observed in the Vfb of the CQDs doped cells in the M-S plot within this experiment.

IPCE has been utilized to provide information about how the quantum efficiency of DSSCs is improved when CQDs are incorporated into the photoanodes. Fig. 5(b) illustrates the IPCE spectra of the cells and observed two peaks on both samples in the 300–800 nm scan range. The corresponding IPCE (%) peak value at 530 nm is presented in Table 1. The peaks observed at ∼338 nm and 530 nm in the UV and visible region indicated the electron excitation in TiO2 under light illumination, and N719 dye absorption (Shahid et al., 2023). The IPCE peak value at 530 nm increases from 17.24 % ± 0.98 % to 19.26 % ± 0.66 % after CQDs incorporation. The photoelectric response outcomes revealed that DSSCs with CQDs exhibit significantly higher IPCE than those without CQDs. Indeed, these findings are consistent with their J SC values, as the IPCE of DSSCs is typically associated with their J SC. Additionally, the long-term stability and performance retention of the DSSCs (with & without CQDs) were investigated to understand their feasibility for practical applications. The results of these tests are reported in the Appendix A Fig. S7.

This study synthesized biomass-derived nitrogen self-doped CQDs from corn stover using a simple hydrothermal technique. The prepared CQDs had an average size with a diameter of 4.63 ± 0.87 nm measured by HR TEM. This approach is cost-effective, unique, and comparatively more eco-friendly, as it omits the use of chemicals, thus presenting a greener alternative that uses agricultural waste. The experimental findings of this study suggested the potential application of CQDs as a sustainable co-sensitizer for the fabrication of TiO2+CQDs photoanodes in DSSCs.

After the addition of CQDs, the fabricated solar cells showed significant improvements in J SC, V OC, and electron transport properties. Based on the CV curve, the energy levels (HOMO & LUMO) of the CQDs were −5.48 eV and −3.89 eV respectively, which matched well with the energy levels of TiO2, N719 dye, and electrolyte. The charge transport resistance was significantly reduced in the CQDs doped cell disclosed by the Nyquist plot. Moreover, the M-S plot depicted a more negative shift in Vfb . The improvement in the overall performance of CQDs co-sensitized DSSCs reported in this study could be caused by the better anchoring of CQDs with TiO2, in which the CQDs serve as mediators for electron transfer to the conduction band of the TiO2 with improved energy band alignment. Thus, utilizing agricultural wastes as carbon sources to fabricate valuable products, our findings could be a baseline for future studies suggesting their potential application in green energy harvesting.
